storytelling in worlds of swords and sorcery. It shares ele-                            ined city, a haunted castle, a lost temple deep in a jungle,
ments with childhood games of make-believe. Like those                                  or a lava-filled cavern beneath a mysterious mountain.
games, D&D is driven by imagination. It’s about picturing                               The adventurers can solve puzzles, talk with other char-
the towering castle beneath the stormy night sky and                                    acters, battle fantastic monsters, and discover fabulous
imagining how a fantasy adventurer might react to the                                   magic items and other treasure.
challenges that scene presents.                                                            One player, however, takes on the role of the Dungeon
                                                                                        Master (DM), the game’s lead storyteller and referee. The
                                                                                        DM creates adventures for the characters, who navigate

 stone gargoyles stare at you from hollow sockets and grin

woven together in the fabric of the multiverse. Alongside
                                                                                       How to Play
these worlds are hundreds of thousands more, created                                   The play of the Dungeons & Dragons game unfolds ac-
by generations of D&D players for their own games. And                                 cording to this basic pattern.
amid all the richness of the multiverse, you might create a                               1. The DM describes the environment. The DM
world of your own.                                                                     tells the players where their adventurers are and what’s
   All these worlds share characteristics, but each world                              around them, presenting the basic scope of options that
is set apart by its own history and cultures, distinctive                              present themselves (how many doors lead out of a room,
monsters and races, fantastic geography, ancient dun-                                  what’s on a table, who’s in the tavern, and so on).
geons, and scheming villains. Some races have unusual                                     2. The players describe what they want to do. Some-
traits in different worlds. The halflings of the Dark Sun                              times one player speaks for the whole party, saying,
setting, for example, are jungle-dwelling cannibals, and                               “We’ll take the east door,” for example. Other times,
the elves are desert nomads. Some worlds feature races                                 different adventurers do different things: one adventurer
unknown in other settings, such as Eberron’s warforged,                                might search a treasure chest while a second examines
soldiers created and imbued with life to fight in the Last                             an esoteric symbol engraved on a wall and a third keeps
War. Some worlds are dominated by one great story, like                                watch for monsters. The players don’t need to take turns,
the War of the Lance that plays a central role in the Drag-                            but the DM listens to every player and decides how to re-
onlance setting. But they’re all D&D worlds, and you can                               solve those actions.
use the rules in this book to create a character and play                                 Sometimes, resolving a task is easy. If an adventurer
in any one of them.                                                                    wants to walk across a room and open a door, the DM
   Your DM might set the campaign on one of these                                      might just say that the door opens and describe what lies
worlds or on one that he or she created. Because there is                              beyond. But the door might be locked, the floor might
so much diversity among the worlds of D&D, you should                                  hide a deadly trap, or some other circumstance might
check with your DM about any house rules that will affect                              make it challenging for an adventurer to complete a task.
your play of the game. Ultimately, the Dungeon Master is                               In those cases, the DM decides what happens, often
the authority on the campaign and its setting, even if the                             relying on the roll of a die to determine the results of
setting is a published world.                                                          an action.

                   The Three Pillars of Adventure                                                           Magic is also a favored tool of villains. Many adven-
                   Adventurers can try to do anything their players can                                   tures are driven by the machinations of spellcasters who
                   imagine, but it can be helpful to talk about their activities                          are hellbent on using magic for some ill end. A cult leader

                   situations that require their attention. Exploration is the                            to rise up as a god of destruction—these are just a few

                   their characters to do, and the Dungeon Master telling                                 magic of their own, in the form of spells and magic items,
                   the players what happens as a result. On a large scale,                                the adventurers might prevail!
                   that might involve the characters spending a day crossing
                   a rolling plain or an hour making their way through cav-
                   erns underground. On the smallest scale, it could mean
                   one character pulling a lever in a dungeon room to see
                   what happens.
                     Social interaction features the adventurers talking to
                   someone (or something) else. It might mean demanding
                   that a captured scout reveal the secret entrance to the
                   goblin lair, getting information from a rescued prisoner,
                   pleading for mercy from an orc chieftain, or persuading
                   a talkative magic mirror to show a distant location to the
                   adventurers.
                     The rules in chapters 7 and 8 support exploration and
                   social interaction, as do many class features in chapter 3
                   and personality traits in chapter 4.
                     Combat, the focus of chapter 9, involves characters
                   and other creatures swinging weapons, casting spells,
                   maneuvering for position, and so on—all in an effort
RICHARD WHITTERS




                                                                                                                                                                      Introduction
                                                                                                                                                                                     6

                                         Character Creation

Chapter 1: Step-by-Step Characters
Your first step in playing an adventurer in the                                         Halfling paladins and mountain dwarf wizards, for exam-
Dungeons & Dragons game is to imagine and create a                                      ple, can be unusual but memorable characters.
character of your own. Your character is a combination                                    Your race also increases one or more of your ability
of game statistics, roleplaying hooks, and your imagina-                                scores, which you determine in step 3. Note these in-
tion. You choose a race (such as human or halfling) and                                 creases and remember to apply them later.
a class (such as fighter or wizard). You also invent the                                  Record the traits granted by your race on your
personality, appearance, and backstory of your character.                               character sheet. Be sure to note your starting
Once completed, your character serves as your repre-                                    languages and your base speed as well.
sentative in the game, your avatar in the Dungeons &
Dragons world.                                                                          Building Bruenor, Step 1
   Before you dive into step 1 below, think about the                                   Bob is sitting down to create his character. He decides
kind of adventurer you want to play. You might be a cou-                                that a gruff mountain dwarf fits the character he wants
rageous fighter, a skulking rogue, a fervent cleric, or a                               to play. He notes all the racial traits of dwarves on his
flamboyant wizard. Or you might be more interested in                                   character sheet, including his speed of 25 feet and the
an unconventional character, such as a brawny rogue                                     languages he knows: Common and Dwarvish.
who likes hand-to-hand combat, or a sharpshooter who
picks off enemies from afar. Do you like fantasy fiction                                2. Choose a Class
featuring dwarves or elves? Try building a character of                                 Every adventurer is a member of a class. Class broadly
one of those races. Do you want your character to be the                                describes a character’s vocation, what special talents he
toughest adventurer at the table? Consider the fighter                                  or she possesses, and the tactics he or she is most likely
class. If you don’t know where else to begin, take a look                               to employ when exploring a dungeon, fighting monsters,
at the illustrations in this book to see what catches                                   or engaging in a tense negotiation. The character classes
your interest.                                                                          are described in chapter 3.
   Once you have a character in mind, follow these steps                                  Your character receives a number of benefits from
in order, making decisions that reflect the character you                               your choice of class. Many of these benefits are class
want. Your conception of your character might evolve                                    features—capabilities (including spellcasting) that set
with each choice you make. What’s important is that you                                 your character apart from members of other classes. You
come to the table with a character you’re excited to play.                              also gain a number of proficiencies: armor, weapons,
   Throughout this chapter, we use the term character                                   skills, saving throws, and sometimes tools. Your profi-
sheet to mean whatever you use to track your character,                                 ciencies define many of the things your character can do
whether it’s a formal character sheet (like the one at the                              particularly well, from using certain weapons to telling a
end of these rules), some form of digital record, or a piece                            convincing lie.
of notebook paper. An official D&D character sheet is a                                   On your character sheet, record all the features that
fine place to start until you know what information you                                 your class gives you at 1st level.
need and how you use it during the game.
                                                                                        Level
Building Bruenor                                                                        Typically, a character starts at 1st level and advances in
Each step of character creation includes an example of                                  level by adventuring and gaining experience points (XP).
that step, with a player named Bob building his dwarf                                   A 1st-level character is inexperienced in the adventuring
character, Bruenor.                                                                     world, although he or she might have been a soldier or a
                                                                                        pirate and done dangerous things before.
1. Choose a Race                                                                          Starting off at 1st level marks your character’s en-
Every character belongs to a race, one of the many in-                                  try into the adventuring life. If you’re already familiar
telligent humanoid species in the D&D world. The most                                   with the game, or if you are joining an existing D&D
common player character races are dwarves, elves,                                       campaign, your DM might decide to have you begin at a
halflings, and humans. Some races also have subraces,                                   higher level, on the assumption that your character has
such as mountain dwarf or wood elf. Chapter 2 provides                                  already survived a few harrowing adventures.
more information about these races.                                                       Record your level on your character sheet. If you’re
  The race you choose contributes to your character’s                                   starting at a higher level, record the additional elements
identity in an important way, by establishing a general                                 your class gives you for your levels past 1st. Also re-
appearance and the natural talents gained from culture                                  cord your experience points. A 1st-level character has
and ancestry. Your character’s race grants particular ra-                               0 XP. A higher-level character typically begins with the
cial traits, such as special senses, proficiency with certain                           minimum amount of XP required to reach that level
weapons or tools, proficiency in one or more skills, or the                             (see “Beyond 1st Level” later in this chapter).
ability to use minor spells. These traits sometimes dove-
tail with the capabilities of certain classes (see step 2). For                         Hit Points and Hit Dice
example, the racial traits of lightfoot halflings make them                             Your character’s hit points define how tough your char-
exceptional rogues, and high elves tend to be powerful                                  acter is in combat and other dangerous situations. Your
wizards. Sometimes playing against type can be fun, too.                                hit points are determined by your Hit Dice (short for Hit
                                                                                        Point Dice).

ful good). His high Strength and Constitution suggest a                               There are drawbacks to wearing armor or carrying a
healthy, athletic body, and his low Intelligence suggests a                           shield if you lack the required proficiency, as explained in
degree of forgetfulness.                                                              chapter 5.
   Bob decides that Bruenor comes from a noble line, but                                Some spells and class features give you a different way
his clan was expelled from its homeland when Bruenor                                  to calculate your AC. If you have multiple features that
was very young. He grew up working as a smith in the re-                              give you different ways to calculate your AC, you choose
mote villages of Icewind Dale. But Bruenor has a heroic                               which one to use.
destiny—to reclaim his homeland—so Bob chooses the
folk hero background for his dwarf. He notes the profi-                               Weapons
ciencies and special feature this background gives him.                               For each weapon your character wields, calculate the
   Bob has a pretty clear picture of Bruenor’s personality                            modifier you use when you attack with the weapon and
in mind, so he skips the personality traits suggested in                              the damage you deal when you hit.
the folk hero background, noting instead that Bruenor is                                When you make an attack with a weapon, you roll
a caring, sensitive dwarf who genuinely loves his friends                             a d20 and add your proficiency bonus (but only if you
and allies, but he hides this soft heart behind a gruff,                              are proficient with the weapon) and the appropriate
snarling demeanor. He chooses the ideal of fairness from                              ability modifier.
the list in his background, noting that Bruenor believes                              • For attacks with melee weapons, use your Strength
that no one is above the law.                                                           modifier for attack and damage rolls. A weapon that
   Given his history, Bruenor’s bond is obvious: he aspires                             has the finesse property, such as a rapier, can use your
to someday reclaim Mithral Hall, his homeland, from the                                 Dexterity modifier instead.
shadow dragon that drove the dwarves out. His flaw is                                 • For attacks with ranged weapons, use your Dexterity
tied to his caring, sensitive nature—he has a soft spot for                             modifier for attack and damage rolls. A melee weapon
orphans and wayward souls, leading him to show mercy                                    that has the thrown property, such as a handaxe, can
even when it might not be warranted.                                                    use your Strength modifier instead.

                   score above 20. In addition, every character’s proficiency                             ning bolt. At this tier, many weapon-using classes gain

                     Each time you gain a level, you gain 1 additional Hit                                characters have become important, facing dangers that
                   Die. Roll that Hit Die, add your Constitution modifier                                 threaten cities and kingdoms.

Chapter 2: Races
A visit to one of the great cities in the worlds of                                    Racial Traits
Dungeons & Dragons—Waterdeep, the Free City of
Greyhawk, or even uncanny Sigil, the City of Doors—                                    The description of each race includes racial traits that are
overwhelms the senses. Voices chatter in countless                                     common to members of that race. The following entries
different languages. The smells of cooking in dozens                                   appear among the traits of most races.
of different cuisines mingle with the odors of crowded
                                                                                       Ability Score Increase
streets and poor sanitation. Buildings in myriad ar-
                                                                                       Every race increases one or more of a character’s abil-
chitectural styles display the diverse origins of their
                                                                                       ity scores.
inhabitants.
   And the people themselves—people of varying size,                                   Age
shape, and color, dressed in a dazzling spectrum of styles                             The age entry notes the age when a member of the race
and hues—represent many different races, from diminu-                                  is considered an adult, as well as the race’s expected
tive halflings and stout dwarves to majestically beautiful                             lifespan. This information can help you decide how old
elves, mingling among a variety of human ethnicities.                                  your character is at the start of the game. You can choose
   Scattered among the members of these more common                                    any age for your character, which could provide an ex-
races are the true exotics: a hulking dragonborn here,                                 planation for some of your ability scores. For example,
pushing his way through the crowd, and a sly tiefling                                  if you play a young or very old character, your age could
there, lurking in the shadows with mischief in her eyes. A                             explain a particularly low Strength or Constitution score,
group of gnomes laughs as one of them activates a clever                               while advanced age could account for a high Intelligence

there, well out of the sunlight, is a lone drow—a fugitive                             Most races have tendencies toward certain alignments,

Humans are the most common people in the worlds of

D&D, but they live and work alongside dwarves, elves,

character belongs to one of these peoples.

ate for a player-controlled adventurer. Dwarves, elves,

halflings, and humans are the most common races to
produce the sort of adventurers who make up typical                                    Speed
parties. Other races and subraces are less common as                                   Your speed determines how far you can move when trav-

   Your choice of race affects many different aspects of
your character. It establishes fundamental qualities that                              Languages
exist throughout your character’s adventuring career.                                  By virtue of your race, your character can speak, read,
When making this decision, keep in mind the kind of                                    and write certain languages. Chapter 4 lists the most
character you want to play. For example, a halfling could                              common languages of the D&D multiverse.
be a good choice for a sneaky rogue, a dwarf makes a

and traits but also provides the cues for building your                                the traits of the parent race in addition to the traits speci-
character’s story. Each race’s description in this chapter                             fied for their subrace. Relationships among subraces vary
includes information to help you roleplay a character of                               significantly from race to race and world to world. In the
that race, including personality, physical appearance, fea-                            Dragonlance campaign setting, for example, mountain

details are suggestions to help you think about your char-                             of the same people, but in the Forgotten Realms, they live
acter; adventurers can deviate widely from the norm for                                far apart in separate kingdoms and call themselves shield
their race. It’s worthwhile to consider why your character                             dwarves and gold dwarves, respectively.
is different, as a helpful way to think about your charac-

“Yer late, elf!” came the rough edge of a familiar                                     Dwarven kingdoms stretch deep beneath the mountains
                                                                                       where the dwarves mine gems and precious metals and
voice. Bruenor Battlehammer walked up the back of his                                  forge items of wonder. They love the beauty and artistry
dead foe, disregarding the fact that the heavy monster lay                             of precious metals and fine jewelry, and in some dwarves
on top of his elven friend. In spite of the added discomfort,                          this love festers into avarice. Whatever wealth they can’t
                                                                                       find in their mountains, they gain through trade. They
the dwarf’s long, pointed, often-broken nose and gray-
                                                                                       dislike boats, so enterprising humans and halflings
streaked though still-fiery red beard came as a welcome                                frequently handle trade in dwarven goods along water
sight to Drizzt. “Knew I’d find ye in trouble if I came out                            routes. Trustworthy members of other races are welcome
an’ looked for ye!”                                                                    in dwarf settlements, though some areas are off limits
                                                                                       even to them.
                       —R. A. Salvatore, The Crystal Shard                                The chief unit of dwarven society is the clan, and
                                                                                       dwarves highly value social standing. Even dwarves
Kingdoms rich in ancient grandeur, halls carved into the                               who live far from their own kingdoms cherish their clan
roots of mountains, the echoing of picks and hammers                                   identities and affiliations, recognize related dwarves, and
in deep mines and blazing forges, a commitment to clan                                 invoke their ancestors’ names in oaths and curses. To be
and tradition, and a burning hatred of goblins and orcs—                               clanless is the worst fate that can befall a dwarf.
these common threads unite all dwarves.                                                   Dwarves in other lands are typically artisans, espe-
                                                                                       cially weaponsmiths, armorers, and jewelers. Some
Short and Stout                                                                        become mercenaries or bodyguards, highly sought after
Bold and hardy, dwarves are known as skilled warriors,                                 for their courage and loyalty.
miners, and workers of stone and metal. Though they
stand well under 5 feet tall, dwarves are so broad and                                 Gods, Gold, and Clan
compact that they can weigh as much as a human standing                                Dwarves who take up the adventuring life might be
nearly two feet taller. Their courage and endurance are                                motivated by a desire for treasure—for its own sake, for
also easily a match for any of the larger folk.                                        a specific purpose, or even out of an altruistic desire to
   Dwarven skin ranges from deep brown to a paler                                      help others. Other dwarves are driven by the command
hue tinged with red, but the most common shades are                                    or inspiration of a deity, a direct calling or simply a desire
light brown or deep tan, like certain tones of earth.                                  to bring glory to one of the dwarf gods. Clan and ancestry
Their hair, worn long but in simple styles, is usually                                 are also important motivators. A dwarf might seek to
black, gray, or brown, though paler dwarves often have                                 restore a clan’s lost honor, avenge an ancient wrong the
red hair. Male dwarves value their beards highly and                                   clan suffered, or earn a new place within the clan after
groom them carefully.
                                                                                           Slow to Trust
Long Memory, Long Grudges                                                                  Dwarves get along passably well with most other races.
Dwarves can live to be more than 400 years old, so the                                     “The difference between an acquaintance and a friend is
oldest living dwarves often remember a very different                                      about a hundred years,” is a dwarf saying that might be
world. For example, some of the oldest dwarves living                                      hyperbole, but certainly points to how difficult it can be
in Citadel Felbarr (in the world of the Forgotten Realms)                                  for a member of a short-lived race like humans to earn a
can recall the day, more than three centuries ago, when                                    dwarf ’s trust.
orcs conquered the fortress and drove them into an exile                                     Elves. “It’s not wise to depend on the elves. No telling
                                                                                           what an elf will do next; when the hammer meets the orc’s
that lasted over 250 years. This longevity grants them a
                                                                                           head, they’re as apt to start singing as to pull out a sword.
perspective on the world that shorter-lived races such as                                  They’re flighty and frivolous. Two things to be said for them,
humans and halflings lack.                                                                 though: They don’t have many smiths, but the ones they
  Dwarves are solid and enduring like the mountains                                        have do very fine work. And when orcs or goblins come
they love, weathering the passage of centuries with stoic                                  streaming down out of the mountains, an elf ’s good to have
endurance and little change. They respect the traditions                                   at your back. Not as good as a dwarf, maybe, but no doubt
of their clans, tracing their ancestry back to the found-                                  they hate the orcs as much as we do.”
ing of their most ancient strongholds in the youth of the                                    Halflings. “Sure, they’re pleasant folk. But show me
world, and don’t abandon those traditions lightly. Part of                                 a halfling hero. An empire, a triumphant army. Even a
those traditions is devotion to the gods of the dwarves,                                   treasure for the ages made by halfling hands. Nothing. How
                                                                                           can you take them seriously?”
who uphold the dwarven ideals of industrious labor, skill
                                                                                             Humans. “You take the time to get to know a human,
in battle, and devotion to the forge.                                                      and by then the human’s on her deathbed. If you’re lucky,
  Individual dwarves are determined and loyal, true to                                     she’s got kin—a daughter or granddaughter, maybe—who’s
their word and decisive in action, sometimes to the point                                  got hands and heart as good as hers. That’s when you can
of stubbornness. Many dwarves have a strong sense of                                       make a human friend. And watch them go! They set their
justice, and they are slow to forget wrongs they have suf-                                 hearts on something, they’ll get it, whether it’s a dragon’s
fered. A wrong done to one dwarf is a wrong done to the                                    hoard or an empire’s throne. You have to admire that
dwarf’s entire clan, so what begins as one dwarf’s hunt                                    kind of dedication, even if it gets them in trouble more
for vengeance can become a full-blown clan feud.                                           often than not.”

having been exiled. Or a dwarf might search for the axe                                    Duergar

centuries ago.                                                                             dwarves. These vicious, stealthy slave traders raid the

Dwarf Names                                                                                races of the Underdark. They have innate magical abilities
                                                                                           to become invisible and to temporarily grow to giant size.
A dwarf’s name is granted by a clan elder, in accordance
with tradition. Every proper dwarven name has been
used and reused down through the generations. A                                          Languages. You can speak, read, and write Common
dwarf’s name belongs to the clan, not to the individual.                               and Dwarvish. Dwarvish is full of hard consonants and
A dwarf who misuses or brings shame to a clan name                                     guttural sounds, and those characteristics spill over into
is stripped of the name and forbidden by law to use any                                whatever other language a dwarf might speak.
dwarven name in its place.                                                               Subrace. Two main subraces of dwarves populate the
                                                                                       worlds of D&D: hill dwarves and mountain dwarves.

  Tordek, Traubon, Travok, Ulfgar, Veit, Vondal                                        remarkable resilience. The gold dwarves of Faerûn in

  Diesa, Eldeth, Falkrunn, Finellen, Gunnloda, Gurdis,                                 the exiled Neidar and the debased Klar of Krynn in the
  Helja, Hlin, Kathra, Kristryd, Ilde, Liftrasa, Mardred,                              Dragonlance setting.
  Riswynn, Sannl, Torbera, Torgga, Vistra                                                Ability Score Increase. Your Wisdom score in-

  Ironfist, Loderr, Lutgehr, Rumnaheim, Strakeln,                                      increases by 1, and it increases by 1 every time you
  Torunn, Ungart                                                                       gain a level.

Elves are a magical people of otherworldly grace, living                                   Haughty but Gracious
in the world but not entirely part of it. They live in places                              Although they can be haughty, elves are generally gracious
of ethereal beauty, in the midst of ancient forests or in                                  even to those who fall short of their high expectations—
silvery spires glittering with faerie light, where soft music                              which is most non-elves. Still, they can find good in just
drifts through the air and gentle fragrances waft on the                                   about anyone.
breeze. Elves love nature and magic, art and artistry, mu-                                   Dwarves. “Dwarves are dull, clumsy oafs. But what they
sic and poetry, and the good things of the world.                                          lack in humor, sophistication, and manners, they make up
                                                                                           in valor. And I must admit, their best smiths produce art
Slender and Graceful                                                                       that approaches elven quality.”
                                                                                             Halflings. “Halflings are people of simple pleasures, and
With their unearthly grace and fine features, elves appear                                 that is not a quality to scorn. They’re good folk, they care
hauntingly beautiful to humans and members of many                                         for each other and tend their gardens, and they have proven
other races. They are slightly shorter than humans on                                      themselves tougher than they seem when the need arises.”
average, ranging from well under 5 feet tall to just over                                    Humans. “All that haste, their ambition and drive to
                                                                                           accomplish something before their brief lives pass away—
6 feet. They are more slender than humans, weighing
                                                                                           human endeavors seem so futile sometimes. But then
only 100 to 145 pounds. Males and females are about
                                                                                           you look at what they have accomplished, and you have to
the same height, and males are only marginally heavier                                     appreciate their achievements. If only they could slow down
than females.                                                                              and learn some refinement.”
   Elves’ coloration encompasses the normal human
range and also includes skin in shades of copper, bronze,
and almost bluish-white, hair of green or blue, and eyes                               Exploration and Adventure
like pools of liquid gold or silver. Elves have no facial and                          Elves take up adventuring out of wanderlust. Since they
little body hair. They favor elegant clothing in bright col-                           are so long-lived, they can enjoy centuries of exploration
ors, and they enjoy simple yet lovely jewelry.                                         and discovery. They dislike the pace of human society,
                                                                                       which is regimented from day to day but constantly
A Timeless Perspective                                                                 changing over decades, so they find careers that let them
Elves can live well over 700 years, giving them a broad                                travel freely and set their own pace. Elves also enjoy
perspective on events that might trouble the shorter-lived                             exercising their martial prowess or gaining greater mag-
races more deeply. They are more often amused than                                     ical power, and adventuring allows them to do so. Some
excited, and more likely to be curious than greedy. They                               might join with rebels fighting against oppression, and
tend to remain aloof and unfazed by petty happenstance.                                others might become champions of moral causes.
When pursuing a goal, however, whether adventuring
on a mission or learning a new skill or art, elves can be                              Elf Names
focused and relentless. They are slow to make friends                                  Elves are considered children until they declare them-
and enemies, and even slower to forget them. They reply                                selves adults, some time after the hundredth birthday,
to petty insults with disdain and to serious insults with                              and before this period they are called by child names.
vengeance.                                                                               On declaring adulthood, an elf selects an adult name,
  Like the branches of a young tree, elves are flexible                                although those who knew him or her as a youngster
in the face of danger. They trust in diplomacy and com-                                might continue to use the child name. Each elf’s adult
promise to resolve differences before they escalate to                                 name is a unique creation, though it might reflect the
violence. They have been known to retreat from intru-                                  names of respected individuals or other family members.
sions into their woodland homes, confident that they can                               Little distinction exists between male names and
simply wait the invaders out. But when the need arises,                                female names; the groupings here reflect only general
elves reveal a stern martial side, demonstrating skill with                            tendencies. In addition, every elf bears a family name,
sword, bow, and strategy.                                                              typically a combination of other Elvish words. Some elves
                                                                                       traveling among humans translate their family names
Hidden Woodland Realms                                                                 into Common, but others retain the Elvish version.
Most elves dwell in small forest villages hidden among                                 Child Names: Ara, Bryn, Del, Eryn, Faen, Innil,
the trees. Elves hunt game, gather food, and grow vege-                                  Lael, Mella, Naill, Naeris, Phann, Rael, Rinn, Sai,
tables, and their skill and magic allow them to support                                  Syllin, Thia, Vall
themselves without the need for clearing and plowing                                   Male Adult Names: Adran, Aelar, Aramil, Arannis,
land. They are talented artisans, crafting finely worked                                 Aust, Beiro, Berrian, Carric, Enialis, Erdan, Erevan,
clothes and art objects. Their contact with outsiders is                                 Galinndan, Hadarai, Heian, Himo, Immeral, Ivellios,
usually limited, though a few elves make a good living by                                Laucian, Mindartis, Paelias, Peren, Quarion, Riardon,
trading crafted items for metals (which they have no inter-                              Rolen, Soveliss, Thamior, Tharivol, Theren, Varis
est in mining).                                                                        Female Adult Names: Adrie, Althaea, Anastrianna,
  Elves encountered outside their own lands are com-                                     Andraste, Antinua, Bethrynna, Birel, Caelynn, Drusilia,
monly traveling minstrels, artists, or sages. Human                                      Enna, Felosial, Ielenia, Jelenneth, Keyleth, Leshanna,
nobles compete for the services of elf instructors to teach                              Lia, Meriele, Mialee, Naivara, Quelenna, Quillathe,
swordplay or magic to their children.                                                    Sariel, Shanairra, Shava, Silaqui, Theirastra, Thia,
                                                                                         Vadania, Valanthe, Xanaphia

Halfling                                                                                   Halflings are easily moved to pity and hate to see any
                                                                                        living thing suffer. They are generous, happily sharing
Regis the halfling, the only one of his kind for                                        what they have even in lean times.
hundreds of miles in any direction, locked his fingers behind
his head and leaned back against the mossy blanket of the
                                                                                        Blend into the Crowd
                                                                                        Halflings are adept at fitting into a community of hu-
tree trunk. Regis was short, even by the standards of his
                                                                                        mans, dwarves, or elves, making themselves valuable
diminutive race, with the fluff of his curly brown locks barely                         and welcome. The combination of their inherent stealth
cresting the three-foot mark, but his belly was amply thick-                            and their unassuming nature helps halflings to avoid un-
ened by his love of a good meal, or several, as the opportu-                            wanted attention.
                                                                                          Halflings work readily with others, and they are loyal
nities presented themselves. The crooked stick that served as                           to their friends, whether halfling or otherwise. They can
his fishing pole rose up above him, clenched between two of                             display remarkable ferocity when their friends, families,
his toes, and hung out over the quiet lake, mirrored perfectly                          or communities are threatened.
in the glassy surface of Maer Dualdon.
                                                                                        Pastoral Pleasantries
                        —R.A. Salvatore, The Crystal Shard
                                                                                        Most halflings live in small, peaceful communities with
                                                                                        large farms and well-kept groves. They rarely build
The comforts of home are the goals of most halflings’                                   kingdoms of their own or even hold much land beyond
lives: a place to settle in peace and quiet, far from ma-                               their quiet shires. They typically don’t recognize any sort
rauding monsters and clashing armies; a blazing fire                                    of halfling nobility or royalty, instead looking to family
and a generous meal; fine drink and fine conversation.                                  elders to guide them. Families preserve their traditional
Though some halflings live out their days in remote ag-                                 ways despite the rise and fall of empires.
ricultural communities, others form nomadic bands that                                    Many halflings live among other races, where the
travel constantly, lured by the open road and the wide                                  halflings’ hard work and loyal outlook offer them abun-
horizon to discover the wonders of new lands and peo-                                   dant rewards and creature comforts. Some halfling
ples. But even these wanderers love peace, food, hearth,                                communities travel as a way of life, driving wagons or
and home, though home might be a wagon jostling along                                   guiding boats from place to place and maintaining no
an dirt road or a raft floating downriver.                                              permanent home.
Small and Practical                                                                     Exploring Opportunities
The diminutive halflings survive in a world full of larger                              Halflings usually set out on the adventurer’s path to de-
creatures by avoiding notice or, barring that, avoiding                                 fend their communities, support their friends, or explore
offense. Standing about 3 feet tall, they appear relatively                             a wide and wonder-filled world. For them, adventuring
harmless and so have managed to survive for centuries                                   is less a career than an opportunity or sometimes a
in the shadow of empires and on the edges of wars and                                   necessity.
political strife. They are inclined to be stout, weighing
between 40 and 45 pounds.                                                               Halfling Names
  Halflings’ skin ranges from tan to pale with a ruddy
cast, and their hair is usually brown or sandy brown and                                A halfling has a given name, a family name, and possibly
wavy. They have brown or hazel eyes. Halfling men often                                 a nickname. Family names are often nicknames that
sport long sideburns, but beards are rare among them                                    stuck so tenaciously they have been passed down through
and mustaches even more so. They like to wear simple,                                   the generations.
comfortable, and practical clothes, favoring bright colors.                             Male Names: Alton, Ander, Cade, Corrin, Eldon, Errich,
  Halfling practicality extends beyond their clothing.                                    Finnan, Garret, Lindal, Lyle, Merric, Milo, Osborn,
They’re concerned with basic needs and simple pleasures                                   Perrin, Reed, Roscoe, Wellby
and have little use for ostentation. Even the wealthiest                                Female Names: Andry, Bree, Callie, Cora, Euphemia,
of halflings keep their treasures locked in a cellar rather                               Jillian, Kithri, Lavinia, Lidda, Merla, Nedda, Paela,
than on display for all to see. They have                                                 Portia, Seraphina, Shaena, Trym, Vani, Verna
a knack for finding the most straightforward solution                                   Family Names: Brushgather, Goodbarrel, Greenbottle,
to a problem, and have little patience for dithering.                                     High-hill, Hilltopple, Leagallow, Tealeaf, Thorngage,
                                                                                          Tosscobble, Underbough
Kind and Curious
Halflings are an affable and cheerful people. They                                      Halfling Traits
cherish the bonds of family and friendship as well                                      Your halfling character has a number of traits in common
as the comforts of hearth and home, harboring few                                       with all other halflings.
dreams of gold or glory. Even adventurers among                                           Ability Score Increase. Your Dexterity score in-
them usually venture into the world for reasons of                                      creases by 2.
community, friendship, wanderlust, or curiosity. They                                     Age. A halfling reaches adulthood at the age of 20
love discovering new things, even simple things, such                                   and generally lives into the middle of his or her sec-
as an exotic food or an unfamiliar style of clothing.                                   ond century.

  Alignment. Most halflings are lawful good. As a rule,                                    Affable and Positive
they are good-hearted and kind, hate to see others in                                      Halflings try to get along with everyone else and are loath to
pain, and have no tolerance for oppression. They are                                       make sweeping generalizations—especially negative ones.
also very orderly and traditional, leaning heavily on                                        Dwarves. “Dwarves make loyal friends, and you can count
the support of their community and the comfort of                                          on them to keep their word. But would it hurt them to smile
their old ways.                                                                            once in a while?”
  Size. Halflings average about 3 feet tall and weigh                                        Elves. “They’re so beautiful! Their faces, their music,
                                                                                           their grace and all. It’s like they stepped out of a wonderful
about 40 pounds. Your size is Small.
                                                                                           dream. But there’s no telling what’s going on behind their
  Speed. Your base walking speed is 25 feet.                                               smiling faces—surely more than they ever let on.”
  Lucky. When you roll a 1 on the d20 for an attack roll,                                    Humans. “Humans are a lot like us, really. At least some
ability check, or saving throw, you can reroll the die and                                 of them are. Step out of the castles and keeps, go talk to
must use the new roll.                                                                     the farmers and herders and you’ll find good, solid folk. Not
  Brave. You have advantage on saving throws against                                       that there’s anything wrong with the barons and soldiers—
being frightened.                                                                          you have to admire their conviction. And by protecting their
  Halfling Nimbleness. You can move through the space                                      own lands, they protect us as well.”
of any creature that is of a size larger than yours.
  Languages. You can speak, read, and write Common
and Halfling. The Halfling language isn’t secret, but                                  Human
halflings are loath to share it with others. They write                                These were the stories of a restless people who
very little, so they don’t have a rich body of literature.
                                                                                       long ago took to the seas and rivers in longboats, first to
Their oral tradition, however, is very strong. Almost all
halflings speak Common to converse with the people                                     pillage and terrorize, then to settle. Yet there was an energy,
in whose lands they dwell or through which they are                                    a love of adventure, that sang from every page. Long into the
traveling.                                                                             night Liriel read, lighting candle after precious candle.
  Subrace. The two main kinds of halfling, lightfoot and
stout, are more like closely related families than true sub-
                                                                                          She’d never given much thought to humans, but these sto-
races. Choose one of these subraces.                                                   ries fascinated her. In these yellowed pages were tales of bold
                                                                                       heroes, strange and fierce animals, mighty primitive gods, and
Lightfoot
                                                                                       a magic that was part and fabric of that distant land.
As a lightfoot halfling, you can easily hide from notice,
even using other people as cover. You’re inclined to be                                           —Elaine Cunningham, Daughter of the Drow
affable and get along well with others. In the Forgotten
Realms, lightfoot halflings have spread the farthest and                               In the reckonings of most worlds, humans are the young-
thus are the most common variety.                                                      est of the common races, late to arrive on the world scene
   Lightfoots are more prone to wanderlust than other hal-                             and short-lived in comparison to dwarves, elves, and
flings, and often dwell alongside other races or take up a                             dragons. Perhaps it is because of their shorter lives that
nomadic life. In the world of Greyhawk, these halflings                                they strive to achieve as much as they can in the years
are called hairfeet or tallfellows.                                                    they are given. Or maybe they feel they have something
   Ability Score Increase. Your Charisma score in-                                     to prove to the elder races, and that’s why they build their
creases by 1.                                                                          mighty empires on the foundation of conquest and trade.
   Naturally Stealthy. You can attempt to hide even when                               Whatever drives them, humans are the innovators, the
you are obscured only by a creature that is at least one                               achievers, and the pioneers of the worlds.
size larger than you.

some resistance to poison. Some say that stouts have

Variety in All Things                                                                      Everyone’s Second-Best Friends
                                                                                           Just as readily as they mix with each other, humans mingle
Humans are the most adaptable and ambitious people                                         with members of other races. They get along with almost
among the common races. They have widely varying                                           everyone, though they might not be close to many. Humans
tastes, morals, and customs in the many different lands                                    serve as ambassadors, diplomats, magistrates, merchants,
where they have settled. When they settle, though, they                                    and functionaries of all kinds.
stay: they build cities to last for the ages, and great king-                                Dwarves. “They’re stout folk, stalwart friends, and true to
doms that can persist for long centuries. An individual                                    their word. Their greed for gold is their downfall, though.”
human might have a relatively short life span, but a hu-                                     Elves. “It’s best not to wander into elven woods. They
man nation or culture preserves traditions with origins                                    don’t like intruders, and you’ll as likely be bewitched as
far beyond the reach of any single human’s memory. They                                    peppered with arrows. Still, if an elf can get past that
                                                                                           damned racial pride and actually treat you like an equal, you
live fully in the present—making them well suited to the
                                                                                           can learn a lot from them.”
adventuring life—but also plan for the future, striving to                                   Halflings. “It’s hard to beat a meal in a halfling home,
leave a lasting legacy. Individually and as a group, hu-                                   as long as you don’t crack your head on the ceiling—good
mans are adaptable opportunists, and they stay alert to                                    food and good stories in front of a nice, warm fire. If
changing political and social dynamics.                                                    halflings had a shred of ambition, they might really amount
                                                                                           to something.”
Lasting Institutions
Where a single elf or dwarf might take on the responsi-                                found in more localized areas of Faerûn. These groups,
bility of guarding a special location or a powerful secret,                            and the typical names of their members, can be used as
humans found sacred orders and institutions for such                                   inspiration no matter which world your human is in.
purposes. While dwarf clans and halfling elders pass on
the ancient traditions to each new generation, human                                   Calishite
temples, governments, libraries, and codes of law fix                                  Shorter and slighter in build than most other humans,
their traditions in the bedrock of history. Humans dream                               Calishites have dusky brown skin, hair, and eyes. They’re
of immortality, but (except for those few who seek un-                                 found primarily in southwest Faerûn.
death or divine ascension to escape death’s clutches) they                             Calishite Names: (Male) Aseir, Bardeid, Haseid,
achieve it by ensuring that they will be remembered when                                Khemed, Mehmen, Sudeiman, Zasheir; (female)
they are gone.                                                                          Atala, Ceidil, Hama, Jasmal, Meilil, Seipora, Yasheira,
  Although some humans can be xenophobic, in general                                    Zasheida; (surnames) Basha, Dumein, Jassan, Khalid,
their societies are inclusive. Human lands welcome large                                Mostana, Pashar, Rein
numbers of nonhumans compared to the proportion of
humans who live in nonhuman lands.                                                     Chondathan
                                                                                       Chondathans are slender, tawny-skinned folk with brown
Exemplars of Ambition                                                                  hair that ranges from almost blond to almost black.
Humans who seek adventure are the most daring and                                      Most are tall and have green or brown eyes, but these
ambitious members of a daring and ambitious race. They                                 traits are hardly universal. Humans of Chondathan
seek to earn glory in the eyes of their fellows by amassing                            descent dominate the central lands of Faerûn, around
power, wealth, and fame. More than other people, hu-                                   the Inner Sea.
mans champion causes rather than territories or groups.                                Chondathan Names: (Male) Darvin, Dorn, Evendur,
                                                                                        Gorstag, Grim, Helm, Malark, Morn, Randal, Stedd;
Human Names and Ethnicities                                                             (female) Arveene, Esvele, Jhessail, Kerri, Lureene, Miri,
Having so much more variety than other cultures, hu-                                    Rowan, Shandri, Tessele; (surnames) Amblecrown,
mans as a whole have no typical names. Some human                                       Buckman, Dundragon, Evenwood, Greycastle, Tallstag
parents give their children names from other languages,
such as Dwarvish or Elvish (pronounced more or less
                                                                                       Damaran
correctly), but most parents give names that are linked to                             Found primarily in the northwest of Faerûn, Damarans
their region’s culture or to the naming traditions of their                            are of moderate height and build, with skin hues ranging
ancestors.                                                                             from tawny to fair. Their hair is usually brown or black,
  The material culture and physical characteristics of                                 and their eye color varies widely, though brown is
humans can change wildly from region to region. In                                     most common.
the Forgotten Realms, for example, the clothing, archi-                                Damaran Names: (Male) Bor, Fodel, Glar, Grigor, Igan,
tecture, cuisine, music, and literature are different in                                Ivor, Kosef, Mival, Orel, Pavel, Sergor; (female) Alethra,
the northwestern lands of the Silver Marches than in                                    Kara, Katernin, Mara, Natali, Olma, Tana, Zora;
distant Turmish or Impiltur to the east—and even more                                   (surnames) Bersk, Chernin, Dotsk, Kulenov, Marsk,
distinctive in far-off Kara-Tur. Human physical character-                              Nemetsk, Shemov, Starag
istics, though, vary according to the ancient migrations
of the earliest humans, so that the humans of the Silver
Marches have every possible variation of coloration
and features.

are widely recognized, though over a dozen others are

                   Tethyrian
                   Widespread along the entire Sword Coast at the west-
                   ern edge of Faerûn, Tethyrians are of medium build
                   and height, with dusky skin that tends to grow fairer the
                   farther north they dwell. Their hair and eye color varies
                   widely, but brown hair and blue eyes are the most com-
                   mon. Tethyrians primarily use Chondathan names.
RICHARD WHITTERS

                                       D&D Basic Rules (Version 1.0). Not for resale. Permission granted to print and photocopy this document for personal use only.
Chapter 3: Classes
Adventurers are extraordinary people, driven by
a thirst for excitement into a life that others would never
                                                                                        Cleric
dare lead. They are heroes, compelled to explore the dark                               Arms and eyes upraised toward the sun and a prayer
places of the world and take on the challenges that lesser                              on his lips, an elf begins to glow with an inner light that
women and men can’t stand against.                                                      spills out to heal his battle-worn companions.
  Class is the primary definition of what your character                                  Chanting a song of glory, a dwarf swings his axe in
can do. It’s more than a profession; it’s your character’s                              wide swaths to cut through the ranks of orcs arrayed
calling. Class shapes the way you think about the world                                 against him, shouting praise to the gods with every
and interact with it and your relationship with other peo-                              foe’s fall.
ple and powers in the multiverse. A fighter, for example,                                 Calling down a curse upon the forces of undeath, a
might view the world in pragmatic terms of strategy and                                 human lifts her holy symbol as light pours from it to drive
maneuvering, and see herself as just a pawn in a much                                   back the zombies crowding in on her companions.
larger game. A cleric, by contrast, might see himself as                                  Clerics are intermediaries between the mortal world
a willing servant in a god’s unfolding plan or a conflict                               and the distant planes of the gods. As varied as the gods
brewing among various deities. While the fighter has con-                               they serve, clerics strive to embody the handiwork of
tacts in a mercenary company or army, the cleric might                                  their deities. No ordinary priest, a cleric is imbued with
know a number of priests, paladins, and devotees who                                    divine magic.
share his faith.
  Your class gives you a variety of special features,                                   Healers and Warriors
such as a fighter’s mastery of weapons and armor, and                                   Divine magic, as the name suggests, is the power of the
a wizard’s spells. At low levels, your class gives you                                  gods, flowing from them into the world. Clerics are con-
only two or three features, but as you advance in level                                 duits for that power, manifesting it as miraculous effects.
you gain more and your existing features often im-                                      The gods don’t grant this power to everyone who seeks it,
prove. Each class entry in this chapter includes a table                                but only to those chosen to fulfill a high calling.
summarizing the benefits you gain at every level, and                                      Harnessing divine magic doesn’t rely on study or train-
a detailed explanation of each one.                                                     ing. A cleric might learn formulaic prayers and ancient
  Adventurers sometimes advance in more than one                                        rites, but the ability to cast cleric spells relies on devotion
class. A rogue might switch direction in life and dab-                                  and an intuitive sense of a deity’s wishes.
ble in the cleric class while continuing to advance as a                                   Clerics combine the helpful magic of healing and in-
rogue. Elves are known to combine martial mastery with                                  spiring their allies with spells that harm and hinder foes.
magical training and advance as fighters and wizards                                    They can provoke awe and dread, lay curses of plague
simultaneously. Optional rules for combining classes in                                 or poison, and even call down flames from heaven to con-
this way, called multiclassing, can be found in the Play-                               sume their enemies. For those evildoers who will benefit
er’s Handbook.                                                                          most from a mace to the head, clerics depend on their
  Four basic classes—listed in the Classes table—are                                    combat training to let them wade into melee with the
found in almost every D&D world and define most typical                                 power of the gods on their side.
adventurers. See the Player’s Handbook for information
about other classes.                                                                    Divine Agents
                                                                                        Not every acolyte or officiant at a temple or shrine is a
                                                                                        cleric. Some priests are called to a simple life of temple
                                                                                        service, carrying out their gods’ will through prayer and

sacrifice, not by magic and strength of arms. In some cit-                              have a special task in mind for you? Or are you striving to
ies, priesthood amounts to a political office, viewed as a                              prove yourself worthy of a great quest?
stepping stone to higher positions of authority and involv-
ing no communion with a god at all. True clerics are rare                               Quick Build
in most hierarchies.                                                                    You can make a cleric quickly by following these sugges-
  When a cleric takes up an adventuring life, it is usually                             tions. First, Wisdom should be your highest ability score,
because his or her god demands it. Pursuing the goals                                   followed by Strength or Constitution. Second, choose the
of the gods often involves braving dangers beyond the                                   acolyte background.
walls of civilization, smiting evil or seeking holy relics in
ancient tombs. Many clerics are also expected to protect
their deities’ worshipers, which can mean fighting ram-
                                                                                        Class Features
                                                                                        As a cleric, you gain the following class features.
paging orcs, negotiating peace between warring nations,
or sealing a portal that would allow a demon prince to                                  Hit Points
enter the world.                                                                        Hit Dice: 1d8 per cleric level
  Most adventuring clerics maintain some connection to                                  Hit Points at 1st Level: 8 + your Constitution modifier
established temples and orders of their faiths. A temple                                Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d8 (or 5) + your
might ask for a cleric’s aid, or a high priest might be in a                             Constitution modifier per cleric level after 1st
position to demand it.
                                                                                        Proficiencies
Creating a Cleric                                                                       Armor: Light armor, medium armor, shields
As you create a cleric, the most important question to                                  Weapons: Simple weapons
consider is which deity to serve and what principles you                                Tools: None
want your character to embody. The Player’s Handbook                                    Saving Throws: Wisdom, Charisma
includes lists of many of the gods of the multiverse.                                   Skills: Choose two from History, Insight, Medicine,
Check with your DM to learn which deities are in                                         Persuasion, and Religion
your campaign.
   Once you’ve chosen a deity, consider your cleric’s rela-                             Equipment
tionship to that god. Did you enter this service willingly?                             You start with the following equipment, in addition to the

The Life domain focuses on the vibrant positive en-
ergy—one of the fundamental forces of the universe—that
sustains all life. The gods of life promote vitality and
health through healing the sick and wounded, caring for
