   Wretched. You live in inhumane conditions. With no                                  are, the greater the chance you will be drawn into politi-
place to call home, you shelter wherever you can, sneak-                               cal intrigue as a pawn or participant.
ing into barns, huddling in old crates, and relying on the
good graces of people better off than you. A wretched                                  Food, Drink, and Lodging
lifestyle presents abundant dangers. Violence, disease,                                The Food, Drink, and Lodging table gives prices for in-
and hunger follow you wherever you go. Other wretched                                  dividual food items and a single night’s lodging. These
people covet your armor, weapons, and adventuring gear,                                prices are included in your total lifestyle expenses.
which represent a fortune by their standards. You are be-
neath the notice of most people.                                                       Food, Drink, and Lodging
   Squalid. You live in a leaky stable, a mud-floored hut
just outside town, or a vermin-infested boarding house                                   Item                                                      Cost
in the worst part of town. You have shelter from the                                     Ale
elements, but you live in a desperate and often violent                                     Gallon                                                  2 sp
environment, in places rife with disease, hunger, and                                       Mug                                                     4 cp
misfortune. You are beneath the notice of most peo-                                      Banquet (per person)                                      10 gp
ple, and you have few legal protections. Most people at                                  Bread, loaf                                                2 cp
this lifestyle level have suffered some terrible setback.                                Cheese, hunk                                               1 sp
They might be disturbed, marked as exiles, or suffer
                                                                                         Inn stay (per day)
from disease.
                                                                                            Squalid                                                 7 cp
   Poor. A poor lifestyle means going without the com-
forts available in a stable community. Simple food and                                      Poor                                                    1 sp
lodgings, threadbare clothing, and unpredictable condi-                                     Modest                                                  5 sp
tions result in a sufficient, though probably unpleasant,                                   Comfortable                                             8 sp
experience. Your accommodations might be a room in                                          Wealthy                                                 2 gp
a flophouse or in the common room above a tavern. You                                       Aristocratic                                            4 gp
benefit from some legal protections, but you still have                                  Meals (per day)
to contend with violence, crime, and disease. People at                                     Squalid                                                 3 cp
this lifestyle level tend to be unskilled laborers, coste-                                  Poor                                                    6 cp
rmongers, peddlers, thieves, mercenaries, and other                                         Modest                                                  3 sp
disreputable types.
                                                                                            Comfortable                                             5 sp
   Modest. A modest lifestyle keeps you out of the slums
                                                                                            Wealthy                                                 8 sp
and ensures that you can maintain your equipment. You
                                                                                            Aristocratic                                            2 gp
live in an older part of town, renting a room in a boarding
house, inn, or temple. You don’t go hungry or thirsty, and                               Meat, chunk                                                3 sp
your living conditions are clean, if simple. Ordinary peo-                               Wine
ple living modest lifestyles include soldiers with families,                                Common (pitcher)                                        2 sp
laborers, students, priests, hedge wizards, and the like.                                   Fine (bottle)                                          10 gp
   Comfortable. Choosing a comfortable lifestyle means
that you can afford nicer clothing and can easily maintain
your equipment. You live in a small cottage in a mid-
                                                                                       Services
dle-class neighborhood or in a private room at a fine inn.                             Adventurers can pay nonplayer characters to assist them
You associate with merchants, skilled tradespeople, and                                or act on their behalf in a variety of circumstances. Most
military officers.                                                                     such hirelings have fairly ordinary skills, while others are
   Wealthy. Choosing a wealthy lifestyle means living a                                masters of a craft or art, and a few are experts with spe-
life of luxury, though you might not have achieved the                                 cialized adventuring skills.
social status associated with the old money of nobility or                               Some of the most basic types of hirelings appear on the
royalty. You live a lifestyle comparable to that of a highly                           Services table. Other common hirelings include any of
successful merchant, a favored servant of the royalty, or                              the wide variety of people who inhabit a typical town or
the owner of a few small businesses. You have respect-                                 city, when the adventurers pay them to perform a specific
able lodgings, usually a spacious home in a good part of                               task. For example, a wizard might pay a carpenter to con-
town or a comfortable suite at a fine inn. You likely have a                           struct an elaborate chest (and its miniature replica) for
small staff of servants.                                                               use in the Leomund’s secret chest spell. A fighter might
   Aristocratic. You live a life of plenty and comfort. You                            commission a blacksmith to forge a special sword. A bard
move in circles populated by the most powerful people                                  might pay a tailor to make exquisite clothing for an up-
in the community. You have excellent lodgings, perhaps                                 coming performance in front of the duke.
a townhouse in the nicest part of town or rooms in the                                   Other hirelings provide more expert or dangerous
finest inn. You dine at the best restaurants, retain the                               services. Mercenary soldiers paid to help the adventur-
most skilled and fashionable tailor, and have servants                                 ers take on a hobgoblin army are hirelings, as are sages
attending to your every need. You receive invitations to                               hired to research ancient or esoteric lore. If a high-level
the social gatherings of the rich and powerful, and spend                              adventurer establishes a stronghold of some kind, he or
evenings in the company of politicians, guild leaders,                                 she might hire a whole staff of servants and agents to run

                   game, and the personal details you create set your char-                               You gain the hit points from your new class as described
                   acter apart from every other character. Even within your                               for levels after 1st. You gain the 1st-level hit points for a
                   class and race, you have options to fine-tune what your                                class only when you are a 1st-level character.
                   character can do. But a few players—with the DM’s per-                                    You add together the Hit Dice granted by all your
                   mission—want to go a step further.                                                     classes to form your pool of Hit Dice. If the Hit Dice are
                     Chapter 6 of the Player’s Handbook defines two                                       the same die type, you can simply pool them together.

                   classes together, and feats are special options you can

                   gain levels. Your DM decides whether these options are                                 Your proficiency bonus is always based on your total
                   available in a campaign.                                                               character level, as shown in the Character Advancement
                                                                                                          table, not your level in a particular class.
                   Multiclassing                                                                          Proficiencies
                   Multiclassing allows you to gain levels in multiple
                   classes. Doing so lets you mix the abilities of those                                  When you gain a level in a class other than your first, you
                   classes to realize a character concept that might not be                               gain only some of that class’s starting proficiencies. See
                   reflected in one of the standard class options.                                        chapter 6 of the Player’s Handbook for more information.
                     With this rule, you have the option of gaining a level
                   in a new class whenever you advance in level, instead                                  Class Features
                   of gaining a level in your current class. Your levels in all                           When you gain a new level in a class, you get its features
                   your classes are added together to determine your char-                                for that level. A few features, however, have additional
                   acter level. For example, if you have three levels in wizard                           rules when you’re multiclassing. See chapter 6 of the
                   and two in fighter, you’re a 5th-level character.                                      Player’s Handbook for more information.
                     As you advance in levels, you might primarily remain
                   a member of your original class with just a few levels in
                   another class, or you might change course entirely, never
                                                                                                          Feats

                   start progressing in a third or fourth class. Compared to                              a character special capabilities. It embodies training, ex-
                   a single-class character of the same level, you’ll sacrifice                           perience, and abilities beyond what a class provides. See
                   some focus in exchange for versatility.                                                chapter 6 of the Player’s Handbook for more information.

                   one, as shown in the Multiclassing Prerequisites table

                   in the Player’s Handbook. Without the full training that
                                                                                                          take that feat. If you ever lose a feat’s prerequisite, you
                   a beginning character receives, you must be a quick
                                                                                                          can’t use that feat until you regain the prerequisite.
                   study in your new class, having a natural aptitude that
                   is reflected by higher-than-average ability scores.

                   Experience Points
                   The experience point cost to gain a level is always based
                   on your total character level, as shown in the Character
                   Advancement table in chapter 1 of this document, not
                   your level in a particular class.

related to that ability.                                                               only one of the dice. You choose which one. For example,

in most abilities. A score of 18 is the highest that a per-                            Lucky trait to reroll the 1.

the total by 2 (round down).                                                           Characters have a proficiency bonus determined by level,

Ability Scores and Modifiers                                                           used in the rules on ability checks, saving throws, and
   Score       Modifier                     Score             Modifier                 attack rolls.
     1           −5                         16–17               +3                        Your proficiency bonus can’t be added to a single die
    2–3          −4                         18–19               +4                     roll or other number more than once. For example, if two
    4–5          −3                         20–21               +5                     different rules say you can add your proficiency bonus to
                                                                                       a Wisdom saving throw, you nevertheless add the bonus
    6–7          −2                         22–23               +6

    8–9          −1                         24–25               +7
                                                                                          Occasionally, your proficiency bonus might be multi-
   10–11         +0                         26–27               +8                     plied or divided (doubled or halved, for example) before
   12–13         +1                         28–29               +9                     you apply it. For example, the rogue’s Expertise feature
   14–15         +2                          30                +10                     doubles the proficiency bonus for certain ability checks.

Normally, your proficiency in a skill applies only to a                                 When a number of individuals are trying to accomplish
specific kind of ability check. Proficiency in Athletics,                               something as a group, the DM might ask for a group
for example, usually applies to Strength checks. In some                                ability check. In such a situation, the characters who are
situations, though, your proficiency might reasonably                                   skilled at a particular task help cover those who aren’t.
apply to a different kind of check. In such cases, the DM                                  To make a group ability check, everyone in the group
might ask for a check using an unusual combination of                                   makes the ability check. If at least half the group
ability and skill, or you might ask your DM if you can ap-                              succeeds, the whole group succeeds. Otherwise, the
ply a proficiency to a different check. For example, if you                             group fails.
have to swim from an offshore island to the mainland,                                      Group checks don’t come up very often, and they’re
your DM might call for a Constitution check to see if you                               most useful when all the characters succeed or fail as a
have the stamina to make it that far. In this case, your                                group. For example, when adventurers are navigating a
DM might allow you to apply your proficiency in Athletics                               swamp, the DM might call for a group Wisdom (Survival)
and ask for a Constitution (Athletics) check. So if you’re                              check to see if the characters can avoid the quicksand,
proficient in Athletics, you apply your proficiency bonus to                            sinkholes, and other natural hazards of the environment.
the Constitution check just as you would normally do for                                If at least half the group succeeds, the successful char-
a Strength (Athletics) check. Similarly, when your dwarf                                acters are able to guide their companions out of danger.
fighter uses a display of raw strength to intimidate an                                 Otherwise, the group stumbles into one of these hazards.
enemy, your DM might ask for a Strength (Intimidation)
check, even though Intimidation is normally associated
with Charisma.
                                                                                        Using Each Ability
                                                                                        Every task that a character or monster might attempt
Passive Checks                                                                          in the game is covered by one of the six abilities. This
                                                                                        section explains in more detail what those abilities mean
A passive check is a special kind of ability check that                                 and the ways they are used in the game.
doesn’t involve any die rolls. Such a check can represent
the average result for a task done repeatedly, such as                                  Strength
searching for secret doors over and over again, or can be
                                                                                        Strength measures bodily power, athletic training, and
used when the DM wants to secretly determine whether
                                                                                        the extent to which you can exert raw physical force.
the characters succeed at something without rolling dice,
such as noticing a hidden monster.                                                      Strength Checks

sive check:                                                                             pull, or break something, to force your body through a
       10 + all modifiers that normally apply to the check                              space, or to otherwise apply brute force to a situation.

                                                                                        Strength checks.
disadvantage, subtract 5. The game refers to a passive

Dexterity                                                                              the following:
Dexterity measures agility, reflexes, and balance.
                                                                                       • Control a heavily laden cart on a steep descent
Dexterity Checks                                                                       • Steer a chariot around a tight turn
A Dexterity check can model any attempt to move nimbly,                                • Pick a lock
quickly, or quietly, or to keep from falling on tricky foot-                           • Disable a trap
ing. The Acrobatics, Sleight of Hand, and Stealth skills                               • Securely tie up a prisoner

your attempt to stay on your feet in a tricky situation, such                          • Craft a small or detailed object
as when you’re trying to run across a sheet of ice, balance
on a tightrope, or stay upright on a rocking ship’s deck.

helps determine the saving throw DCs of spells they cast.

Chapter 8: Adventuring
Delving into the ancient Tomb of Horrors, slipping
through the back alleys of Waterdeep, hacking a fresh
                                                                                      Movement
trail through the thick jungles on the Isle of Dread—these                            Swimming across a rushing river, sneaking down a dun-
are the things that Dungeons & Dragons adventures                                     geon corridor, scaling a treacherous mountain slope—all
are made of. Your character in the game might explore                                 sorts of movement play a key role in D&D adventures.
forgotten ruins and uncharted lands, uncover dark se-                                    The DM can summarize the adventurers’ movement
crets and sinister plots, and slay foul monsters. And if                              without calculating exact distances or travel times: “You
all goes well, your character will survive to claim rich                              travel through the forest and find the dungeon entrance
rewards before embarking on a new adventure.                                          late in the evening of the third day.” Even in a dungeon,
  This chapter covers the basics of the adventuring life,                             particularly a large dungeon or a cave network, the DM
from the mechanics of movement to the complexities of                                 can summarize movement between encounters: “After
social interaction. The rules for resting are also in this                            killing the guardian at the entrance to the ancient dwar-
chapter, along with a discussion of the activities your                               ven stronghold, you consult your map, which leads you
character might pursue between adventures.                                            through miles of echoing corridors to a chasm bridged by
  Whether adventurers are exploring a dusty dungeon                                   a narrow stone arch.”
or the complex relationships of a royal court, the game                                  Sometimes it’s important, though, to know how long it
follows a natural rhythm, as outlined in the book’s                                   takes to get from one spot to another, whether the answer
introduction:                                                                         is in days, hours, or minutes. The rules for determining
                                                                                      travel time depend on two factors: the speed and travel
1. The DM describes the environment.                                                  pace of the creatures moving and the terrain they’re
2. The players describe what they want to do.                                         moving over.
3. The DM narrates the results of their actions.
Typically, the DM uses a map as an outline of the adven-                              Speed
ture, tracking the characters’ progress as they explore                               Every character and monster has a speed, which is the
dungeon corridors or wilderness regions. The DM’s                                     distance in feet that the character or monster can walk in
notes, including a key to the map, describe what the ad-                              1 round. This number assumes short bursts of energetic
venturers find as they enter each new area. Sometimes,                                movement in the midst of a life-threatening situation.
the passage of time and the adventurers’ actions deter-                                 The following rules determine how far a character
mine what happens, so the DM might use a timeline or a                                or monster can move in a minute, an hour, or a day.
flowchart to track their progress instead of a map.

is important, the DM determines the time a task requires.

ronment, the adventurers’ movement happens on a scale

of minutes. It takes them about a minute to creep down

a long hallway, another minute to check for traps on

the door at the end of the hall, and a good ten minutes

to search the chamber beyond for anything interesting

or valuable.

ambush interrupts their journey.

relies on rounds, a 6-second span of time described in

chapter 9.

relatively simple terrain: roads, open plains, or clear dun-                            the group’s journey.
geon corridors. But adventurers often face dense forests,
                                                                                        Marching Order
deep swamps, rubble-filled ruins, steep mountains, and
                                                                                        The adventurers should establish a marching order. A
ice-covered ground—all considered difficult terrain.
                                                                                        marching order makes it easier to determine which char-
  You move at half speed in difficult terrain—moving
                                                                                        acters are affected by traps, which ones can spot hidden
1 foot in difficult terrain costs 2 feet of speed—so you

an hour, or a day.
                                                                                           A character might occupy the front rank, one or more
Special Types of Movement                                                               middle ranks, or the back rank. Characters in the front
                                                                                        and back ranks need enough room to travel side by side
Movement through dangerous dungeons or wilderness                                       with others in their rank. When space is too tight, the
areas often involves more than simply walking. Adven-                                   marching order must change, usually by moving charac-
turers might have to climb, crawl, swim, or jump to get                                 ters to a middle rank.
where they need to go.                                                                     Fewer Than Three Ranks. If an adventuring party
                                                                                        arranges its marching order with only two ranks, they
Climbing, Swimming, and Crawling                                                        are a front rank and a back rank. If there’s only one rank,
Each foot of movement costs 1 extra foot (2 extra feet                                  it’s considered a front rank.
in difficult terrain) when you’re climbing, swimming, or
crawling. You ignore this extra cost if you have a climbing                             Stealth
speed and use it to climb or a swimming speed and use it                                While traveling at a slow pace, the characters can move
to swim. At the DM’s option, climbing a slippery vertical                               stealthily. As long as they’re not in the open, they can try
surface or one with few handholds requires a successful                                 to surprise or sneak by other creatures they encounter.
Strength (Athletics) check. Similarly, gaining any dis-                                 See the rules for hiding in chapter 7.
tance in rough water might require a successful Strength
(Athletics) check.
                                                                                            Splitting Up the Party
Jumping                                                                                     Sometimes, it makes sense to split an adventuring party,
Your Strength determines how far you can jump.                                              especially if you want one or more characters to scout
                                                                                            ahead. You can form multiple parties, each moving at a
  Long Jump. When you make a long jump, you cover a
                                                                                            different speed. Each group has its own front, middle,
number of feet up to your Strength score if you move at                                     and back ranks.
least 10 feet on foot immediately before the jump. When                                       The drawback to this approach is that the party will be
you make a standing long jump, you can leap only half                                       split into several smaller groups in the event of an attack.
that distance. Either way, each foot you clear on the jump                                  The advantage is that a small group of stealthy characters
costs a foot of movement.                                                                   moving slowly might be able to sneak past enemies that
  This rule assumes that the height of your jump doesn’t                                    clumsier characters would alert. A pair of rogues moving at
matter, such as a jump across a stream or chasm. At                                         a slow pace are much harder to detect when they leave their
your DM’s option, you must succeed on a DC 10 Strength                                      dwarf fighter friend behind.
(Athletics) check to clear a low obstacle (no taller than a
quarter of the jump’s distance), such as a hedge or low
wall. Otherwise, you hit it.

some other activity with the DM’s permission.                                            The presence or absence of light in an environment

when the DM calls for it. (The Dungeon Master’s Guide                                    Bright light lets most creatures see normally. Even

                                                                                      Blindsight
The Environment                                                                       A creature with blindsight can perceive its surroundings
By its nature, adventuring involves delving into places                               without relying on sight, within a specific radius. Crea-
that are dark, dangerous, and full of mysteries to be ex-                             tures without eyes, such as oozes, and creatures with
plored. The rules in this section cover some of the most                              echolocation or heightened senses, such as bats and true
important ways in which adventurers interact with the en-                             dragons, have this sense.
vironment in such places. The Dungeon Master’s Guide
has rules covering more unusual situations.                                           Darkvision
                                                                                      Many creatures in the worlds of D&D, especially those
Falling                                                                               that dwell underground, have darkvision. Within a speci-
                                                                                      fied range, a creature with darkvision can see in dim light
A fall from a great height is one of the most common haz-

ards facing an adventurer.
                                                                                      light, so areas of darkness are only lightly obscured as far

damage for every 10 feet it fell, to a maximum of 20d6.
                                                                                      discern color in darkness, only shades of gray.

                                                                                       Social Interaction
                                                                                       Exploring dungeons, overcoming obstacles, and slay-
the creature can see into the Ethereal Plane.                                          ing monsters are key parts of D&D adventures. No less
                                                                                       important, though, are the social interactions that adven-
Food and Water                                                                         turers have with other inhabitants of the world.
Characters who don’t eat or drink suffer the effects of                                   Interaction takes on many forms. You might need to
exhaustion (see appendix A). Exhaustion caused by lack                                 convince an unscrupulous thief to confess to some mal-
of food or water can’t be removed until the character eats                             feasance, or you might try to flatter a dragon so that it
and drinks the full required amount.                                                   will spare your life. The DM assumes the roles of any
                                                                                       characters who are participating in the interaction that
Food                                                                                   don’t belong to another player at the table. Any such char-
A character needs one pound of food per day and can                                    acter is called a nonplayer character (NPC).
make food last longer by subsisting on half rations.                                      In general terms, an NPC’s attitude toward you is de-
Eating half a pound of food in a day counts as half a day                              scribed as friendly, indifferent, or hostile. Friendly NPCs
without food.                                                                          are predisposed to help you, and hostile ones are inclined
  A character can go without food for a number of days                                 to get in your way. It’s easier to get what you want from a
equal to 3 + his or her Constitution modifier (minimum                                 friendly NPC, of course.
1). At the end of each day beyond that limit, a character                                 Social interactions have two primary aspects: roleplay-
automatically suffers one level of exhaustion.                                         ing and ability checks.
   A normal day of eating resets the count of days without
food to zero.                                                                          Roleplaying
Water                                                                                  Roleplaying is, literally, the act of playing out a role. In
A character needs one gallon of water per day, or two gal-                             this case, it’s you as a player determining how your char-
lons per day if the weather is hot. A character who drinks                             acter thinks, acts, and talks.
only half that much water must succeed on a DC 15 Con-                                   Roleplaying is a part of every aspect of the game, and it
stitution saving throw or suffer one level of exhaustion at                            comes to the fore during social interactions. Your charac-
the end of the day. A character with access to even less                               ter’s quirks, mannerisms, and personality influence how
water automatically suffers one level of exhaustion at the                             interactions resolve.
end of the day.                                                                          There are two styles you can use when roleplaying your
  If the character already has one or more levels of ex-                               character: the descriptive approach and the active ap-
haustion, the character takes two levels in either case.                               proach. Most players use a combination of the two styles.
                                                                                       Use whichever mix of the two works best for you.
Interacting with Objects                                                               Descriptive Approach to Roleplaying
A character’s interaction with objects in an environment                               With this approach, you describe your character’s words
is often simple to resolve in the game. The player tells the                           and actions to the DM and the other players. Drawing on
DM that his or her character is doing something, such                                  your mental image of your character, you tell everyone
as moving a lever, and the DM describes what, if any-                                  what your character does and how he or she does it.
thing, happens.                                                                          For instance, Chris plays Tordek the dwarf. Tordek has
   For example, a character might decide to pull a lever,                              a quick temper and blames the elves of the Cloakwood
which might, in turn, raise a portcullis, cause a room to                              for his family’s misfortune. At a tavern, an obnoxious elf
flood with water, or open a secret door in a nearby wall. If                           minstrel sits at Tordek’s table and tries to strike up a con-
the lever is rusted in position, though, a character might                             versation with the dwarf.
need to force it. In such a situation, the DM might call                                 Chris says, “Tordek spits on the floor, growls an insult
for a Strength check to see whether the character can                                  at the bard, and stomps over to the bar. He sits on a stool
wrench the lever into place. The DM sets the DC for any                                and glares at the minstrel before ordering another drink.”
such check based on the difficulty of the task.                                          In this example, Chris has conveyed Tordek’s mood
   Characters can also damage objects with their weap-                                 and given the DM a clear idea of his character’s attitude
ons and spells. Objects are immune to poison and                                       and actions.
psychic damage, but otherwise they can be affected by                                    When using descriptive roleplaying, keep the following
physical and magical attacks much like creatures can.                                  things in mind:
The DM determines an object’s Armor Class and hit
points, and might decide that certain objects have resis-                              • Describe your character’s emotions and attitude.
tance or immunity to certain kinds of attacks. (It’s hard                              • Focus on your character’s intent and how others
to cut a rope with a club, for example.) Objects always                                  might perceive it.
fail Strength and Dexterity saving throws, and they are                                • Provide as much embellishment as you feel
                                                                                         comfortable with.

  Don’t worry about getting things exactly right. Just fo-
cus on thinking about what your character would do and
                                                                                       Resting
describing what you see in your mind.                                                  Heroic though they might be, adventurers can’t spend
                                                                                       every hour of the day in the thick of exploration, social in-
Active Approach to Roleplaying                                                         teraction, and combat. They need rest—time to sleep and
If descriptive roleplaying tells your DM and your fellow                               eat, tend their wounds, refresh their minds and spirits for
players what your character thinks and does, active role-                              spellcasting, and brace themselves for further adventure.
playing shows them.                                                                      Adventurers, as well as other creatures, can take short
   When you use active roleplaying, you speak with your                                rests in the midst of a day and a long rest to end it.
character’s voice, like an actor taking on a role. You might
even echo your character’s movements and body language.                                Short Rest
This approach is more immersive than descriptive role-                                 A short rest is a period of downtime, at least 1 hour long,
playing, though you still need to describe things that                                 during which a character does nothing more strenuous
can’t be reasonably acted out.                                                         than eating, drinking, reading, and tending to wounds.
   Going back to the example of Chris roleplaying Tordek                                  A character can spend one or more Hit Dice at the end
above, here’s how the scene might play out if Chris used                               of a short rest, up to the character’s maximum number of
active roleplaying:                                                                    Hit Dice, which is equal to the character’s level. For each
   Speaking as Tordek, Chris says in a gruff, deep voice,                              Hit Die spent in this way, the player rolls the die and adds
“I was wondering why it suddenly smelled awful in here.                                the character’s Constitution modifier to it. The character
If I wanted to hear anything out of you, I’d snap your arm                             regains hit points equal to the total (minimum of 0). The
and enjoy your screams.” In his normal voice, Chris then                               player can decide to spend an additional Hit Die after
adds, “I get up, glare at the elf, and head to the bar.”                               each roll. A character regains some spent Hit Dice upon
                                                                                       finishing a long rest, as explained below.
Results of Roleplaying
The DM uses your character’s actions and attitudes to                                  Long Rest
determine how an NPC reacts. A cowardly NPC buckles
under threats of violence. A stubborn dwarf refuses to let                             A long rest is a period of extended downtime, at least 8
anyone badger her. A vain dragon laps up flattery.                                     hours long, during which a character sleeps for at least
   When interacting with an NPC, pay close attention to                                6 hours and performs no more than 2 hours of light ac-
the DM’s portrayal of the NPC’s mood, dialogue, and                                    tivity, such as reading, talking, eating, or standing watch.
personality. You might be able to determine an NPC’s                                   If the rest is interrupted by a period of strenuous activ-
personality traits, ideals, flaws, and bonds, then play on                             ity—at least 1 hour of walking, fighting, casting spells, or
them to influence the NPC’s attitude.                                                  similar adventuring activity—the characters must begin
   Interactions in D&D are much like interactions in real                              the rest again to gain any benefit from it.
life. If you can offer NPCs something they want, threaten                                 At the end of a long rest, a character regains all lost
them with something they fear, or play on their sympa-                                 hit points. The character also regains spent Hit Dice, up
thies and goals, you can use words to get almost anything                              to a number of dice equal to half of the character’s total
you want. On the other hand, if you insult a proud warrior                             number of them (minimum of one die). For example, if a
or speak ill of a noble’s allies, your efforts to convince or                          character has eight Hit Dice, he or she can regain four
deceive will fall short.                                                               spent Hit Dice upon finishing a long rest.
                                                                                          A character can’t benefit from more than one long rest
                                                                                       in a 24-hour period, and a character must have at least
Ability Checks                                                                         1 hit point at the start of the rest to gain its benefits.
In addition to roleplaying, ability checks are key in deter-
mining the outcome of an interaction.
   Your roleplaying efforts can alter an NPC’s attitude, but                           Between Adventures
there might still be an element of chance in the situation.                            Between trips to dungeons and battles against ancient

any point during an interaction if he or she wants the dice                            prepare for their next adventure. Many adventurers also
to play a role in determining an NPC’s reactions. Other                                use this time to perform other tasks, such as crafting
checks might be appropriate in certain situations, at your                             arms and armor, performing research, or spending
DM’s discretion.                                                                       their hard-earned gold.
   Pay attention to your skill proficiencies when thinking                               In some cases, the passage of time is something that
of how you want to interact with an NPC, and stack the                                 occurs with little fanfare or description. When starting a
deck in your favor by using an approach that relies on                                 new adventure, the DM might simply declare that a cer-
your best bonuses and skills. If the group needs to trick a                            tain amount of time has passed and allow you to describe
guard into letting them into a castle, the rogue who is pro-                           in general terms what your character has
ficient in Deception is the best bet to lead the discussion.                           been doing. At other times, the DM might want to keep

                                                                                       vide gainful employment, such as a temple or a thieves’

life and pay the cost of maintaining that lifestyle, as de-                            style instead.
scribed in chapter 5.                                                                    If you have proficiency in the Performance skill and put
   Living a particular lifestyle doesn’t have a huge effect                            your performance skill to use during your downtime, you
on your character, but your lifestyle can affect the way                               earn enough to support a wealthy lifestyle instead.
other individuals and groups react to you. For example,
when you lead an aristocratic lifestyle, it might be easier                            Recuperating
for you to influence the nobles of the city than if you live                           You can use downtime between adventures to recover
in poverty.                                                                            from a debilitating injury, disease, or poison.

Downtime Activities                                                                    you can make a DC 15 Constitution saving throw.
Between adventures, the DM might ask you what your                                     On a successful save, you can choose one of the
character is doing during his or her downtime. Periods                                 following results:
of downtime can vary in duration, but each downtime                                    • End one effect on you that prevents you from regain-
activity requires a certain number of days to complete                                   ing hit points.
before you gain any benefit, and at least 8 hours of each                              • For the next 24 hours, gain advantage on saving throws
day must be spent on the downtime activity for the day                                   against one disease or poison currently
to count. The days do not need to be consecutive. If you                                 affecting you.
have more than the minimum amount of days to spend,
you can keep doing the same thing for a longer period                                  Researching
of time, or switch to a new downtime activity.                                         The time between adventures is a great chance to per-
  Downtime activities other than the ones presented be-                                form research, gaining insight into mysteries that have
low are possible. If you want your character to spend his                              unfurled over the course of the campaign. Research can
or her downtime performing an activity not covered here,                               include poring over dusty tomes and crumbling scrolls
discuss it with your DM.                                                               in a library or buying drinks for the locals to pry rumors
                                                                                       and gossip from their lips.
Crafting                                                                                  When you begin your research, the DM determines
You can craft nonmagical objects, including adventur-                                  whether the information is available, how many days of
ing equipment and works of art. You must be proficient                                 downtime it will take to find it, and whether there are any
with tools related to the object you are trying to create                              restrictions on your research (such as needing to seek out
(typically artisan’s tools). You might also need access to                             a specific individual, tome, or location). The DM might
special materials or locations necessary to create it. For                             also require you to make one or more ability checks,
example, someone proficient with smith’s tools needs a                                 such as an Intelligence (Investigation) check to find clues
forge in order to craft a sword or suit of armor.                                      pointing toward the information you seek, or a Charisma
   For every day of downtime you spend crafting, you can                               (Persuasion) check to secure someone’s aid. Once those
craft one or more items with a total market value not ex-                              conditions are met, you learn the information if it is
ceeding 5 gp, and you must expend raw materials worth                                  available.
half the total market value. If something you want to craft                               For each day of research, you must spend 1 gp to cover
has a market value greater than 5 gp, you make progress                                your expenses. This cost is in addition to your normal
every day in 5-gp increments until you reach the market                                lifestyle expenses (as discussed in chapter 5).
value of the item. For example, a suit of plate armor (mar-
ket value 1,500 gp) takes 300 days to craft by yourself.                               Training
   Multiple characters can combine their efforts toward                                You can spend time between adventures learning a new
the crafting of a single item, provided that the charac-                               language or training with a set of tools. Your DM might
ters all have proficiency with the requisite tools and are                             allow additional training options.
working together in the same place. Each character con-                                  First, you must find an instructor willing to teach you.
tributes 5 gp worth of effort for every day spent helping to                           The DM determines how long it takes, and whether one
craft the item. For example, three characters with the req-                            or more ability checks are required.
uisite tool proficiency and the proper facilities can craft a                            The training lasts for 250 days and costs 1 gp per day.
suit of plate armor in 100 days, at a total cost of 750 gp.                            After you spend the requisite amount of time and money,
   While crafting, you can maintain a modest lifestyle                                 you learn the new language or gain proficiency with
without having to pay 1 gp per day, or a comfortable                                   the new tool.
lifestyle at half the normal cost (see chapter 5 for more
information on lifestyle expenses).

Practicing a Profession
You can work between adventures, allowing you to main-
tain a modest lifestyle without having to pay 1 gp per day
(see chapter 5 for more information on lifestyle expenses).
This benefit lasts as long you continue to practice your
profession.

Chapter 9: Combat
The clatter of a sword striking against a shield.                                       side. Any character or monster that doesn’t notice a
The terrible rending sound as monstrous claws tear                                      threat is surprised at the start of the encounter.
through armor. A brilliant flash of light as a ball of flame                              If you’re surprised, you can’t move or take an action
blossoms from a wizard’s spell. The sharp tang of blood                                 on your first turn of the combat, and you can’t take a re-
in the air, cutting through the stench of vile monsters.                                action until that turn ends. A member of a group can be
Roars of fury, shouts of triumph, cries of pain. Combat in                              surprised even if the other members aren’t.
D&D can be chaotic, deadly, and thrilling.
  This chapter provides the rules you need for your char-                               Initiative
acters and monsters to engage in combat, whether it is                                  Initiative determines the order of turns during combat.
a brief skirmish or an extended conflict in a dungeon or                                When combat starts, every participant makes a Dexterity
on a field of battle. Throughout this chapter, the rules ad-                            check to determine their place in the initiative order. The
dress you, the player or Dungeon Master. The Dungeon                                    DM makes one roll for an entire group of identical crea-
Master controls all the monsters and nonplayer charac-                                  tures, so each member of the group acts at the same time.
ters involved in combat, and each other player controls an                                The DM ranks the combatants in order from the one
adventurer. “You” can also mean the character or mon-                                   with the highest Dexterity check total to the one with
ster that you control.                                                                  the lowest. This is the order (called the initiative order)
                                                                                        in which they act during each round. The initiative order
The Order of Combat                                                                     remains the same from round to round.
A typical combat encounter is a clash between two sides,                                  If a tie occurs, the DM decides the order among tied
a flurry of weapon swings, feints, parries, footwork, and                               DM-controlled creatures, and the players decide the or-
spellcasting. The game organizes the chaos of combat                                    der among their tied characters. The DM can decide the
into a cycle of rounds and turns. A round represents                                    order if the tie is between a monster and a player charac-
about 6 seconds in the game world. During a round, each                                 ter. Optionally, the DM can have the tied characters and
participant in a battle takes a turn. The order of turns is                             monsters each roll a d20 to determine the order, highest
determined at the beginning of a combat encounter, when                                 roll going first.
everyone rolls initiative. Once everyone has taken a turn,
the fight continues to the next round if neither side has                               Your Turn
defeated the other.                                                                     On your turn, you can move a distance up to your speed
                                                                                        and take one action. You decide whether to move first or
Surprise                                                                                take your action first. Your speed—sometimes called your
A band of adventurers sneaks up on a bandit camp,                                       walking speed—is noted on your character sheet.
springing from the trees to attack them. A gelatinous                                     The most common actions you can take are described
cube glides down a dungeon passage, unnoticed by the                                    in the “Actions in Combat” section later in this chapter.
adventurers until the cube engulfs one of them. In these                                Many class features and other abilities provide additional
situations, one side of the battle gains surprise over                                  options for your action.
the other.                                                                                The “Movement and Position” section later in this chap-
  The DM determines who might be surprised. If nei-                                     ter gives the rules for your move.
ther side tries to be stealthy, they automatically notice                                 You can forgo moving, taking an action, or doing any-
each other. Otherwise, the DM compares the Dexterity                                    thing at all on your turn. If you can’t decide what to do on
(Stealth) checks of anyone hiding with the passive Wis-                                 your turn, consider taking the Dodge or Ready action, as
dom (Perception) score of each creature on the opposing                                 described in “Actions in Combat.”

                                                                                        Bonus Actions
  Combat Step by Step                                                                   Various class features, spells, and other abilities let you
  1. Determine surprise. The DM determines whether anyone                               take an additional action on your turn called a bonus ac-
     involved in the combat encounter is surprised.                                     tion. The Cunning Action feature, for example, allows a
  2. Establish positions. The DM decides where all the                                  rogue to take a bonus action. You can take a bonus action
     characters and monsters are located. Given the                                     only when a special ability, spell, or other feature of the
     adventurers’ marching order or their stated positions in
                                                                                        game states that you can do something as a bonus action.
     the room or other location, the DM figures out where the
                                                                                        You otherwise don’t have a bonus action to take.
     adversaries are—how far away and in what direction.
                                                                                           You can take only one bonus action on your turn, so you
  3. Roll initiative. Everyone involved in the combat
                                                                                        must choose which bonus action to use when you have
     encounter rolls initiative, determining the order of
     combatants’ turns.                                                                 more than one available.
  4. Take turns. Each participant in the battle takes a turn in                            You choose when to take a bonus action during your
     initiative order.                                                                  turn, unless the bonus action’s timing is specified, and
  5. Begin the next round. When everyone involved in the                                anything that deprives you of your ability to take actions
     combat has had a turn, the round ends. Repeat step 4                               also prevents you from taking a bonus action.
     until the fighting stops.

Other Activity on Your Turn                                                               On your turn, you can move a distance up to your
Your turn can include a variety of flourishes that require                              speed. You can use as much or as little of your speed as
neither your action nor your move.                                                      you like on your turn, following the rules here.
  You can communicate however you are able, through                                       Your movement can include jumping, climbing, and
brief utterances and gestures, as you take your turn.                                   swimming. These different modes of movement can be
  You can also interact with one object or feature of the                               combined with walking, or they can constitute your entire
environment for free, during either your move or your                                   move. However you’re moving, you deduct the distance of

to use your action. Some magic items and other special                                  and swimming.
objects always require an action to use, as stated

  The DM might require you to use an action for any                                     You can break up your movement on your turn, using
of these activities when it needs special care or when it                               some of your speed before and after your action. For
presents an unusual obstacle. For instance, the DM                                      example, if you have a speed of 30 feet, you can move
could reasonably expect you to use an action to open a                                  10 feet, take your action, and then move 20 feet.
stuck door or turn a crank to lower a drawbridge.

Reactions                                                                               If you take an action that includes more than one weapon
Certain special abilities, spells, and situations allow you                             attack, you can break up your movement even further by
to take a special action called a reaction. A reaction is                               moving between those attacks. For example, a fighter
an instant response to a trigger of some kind, which can                                who can make two attacks with the Extra Attack feature
occur on your turn or on someone else’s. The opportunity                                and who has a speed of 25 feet could move 10 feet, make
attack, described later in this chapter, is the most com-                               an attack, move 15 feet, and then attack again.
mon type of reaction.
  When you take a reaction, you can’t take another one

until the start of your next turn. If the reaction interrupts
                                                                                        speed and a flying speed, you can switch back and forth
another creature’s turn, that creature can continue its

turn right after the reaction.

Movement and Position                                                                   you can move. If the result is 0 or less, you can’t use the
In combat, characters and monsters are in constant                                      new speed during the current move.
motion, often using movement and position to gain the                                      For example, if you have a speed of 30 and a flying
upper hand.                                                                             speed of 60 because a wizard cast the fly spell on you,

  Interacting with Objects Around You                                                   the air to fly 30 feet more.
  Here are a few examples of the sorts of thing you can do in
  tandem with your movement and action:                                                 Difficult Terrain
  • draw or sheathe a sword                                                             Combat rarely takes place in bare rooms or on fea-
  • open or close a door                                                                tureless plains. Boulder-strewn caverns, briar-choked
  • withdraw a potion from your backpack                                                forests, treacherous staircases—the setting of
  • pick up a dropped axe                                                               a typical fight contains difficult terrain.
  • take a bauble from a table                                                            Every foot of movement in difficult terrain costs 1
  • remove a ring from your finger                                                      extra foot. This rule is true even if multiple things in
  • stuff some food into your mouth                                                     a space count as difficult terrain.
  • plant a banner in the ground                                                          Low furniture, rubble, undergrowth, steep stairs, snow,
  • fish a few coins from your belt pouch
                                                                                        and shallow bogs are examples of difficult terrain. The
  • drink all the ale in a flagon
  • throw a lever or a switch                                                           space of another creature, whether hostile or not, also
  • pull a torch from a sconce                                                          counts as difficult terrain.
  • take a book from a shelf you can reach
  • extinguish a small flame                                                            Being Prone
  • don a mask                                                                          Combatants often find themselves lying on the ground,
  • pull the hood of your cloak up and over your head
                                                                                        either because they are knocked down or because they
  • put your ear to a door
  • kick a small stone
                                                                                        throw themselves down. In the game, they are prone,
  • turn a key in a lock                                                                a condition described in appendix A.
  • tap the floor with a 10-foot pole                                                     You can drop prone without using any of your speed.
  • hand an item to another character                                                   Standing up takes more effort; doing so costs an
                                                                                        amount of movement equal to half your speed. For ex-
                                                                                        ample, if your speed is 30 feet, you must spend 15 feet

Size            Space                         Example Monsters                             or other tokens, follow these rules.
                                                                                              Squares. Each square on the grid represents 5 feet.
Tiny            2½ by 2½ ft.                  Imp, sprite
                                                                                              Speed. Rather than moving foot by foot, move square by
Small           5 by 5 ft.                    Giant rat, goblin                            square on the grid. This means you use your speed in 5-foot
Medium          5 by 5 ft.                    Orc, werewolf                                segments. This is particularly easy if you translate your
Large           10 by 10 ft.                  Hippogriff, ogre                             speed into squares by dividing the speed by 5. For example,
Huge            15 by 15 ft.                  Fire giant, treant                           a speed of 30 feet translates into a speed of 6 squares.
Gargantuan      20 by 20 ft. or larger        Kraken, purple worm                             If you use a grid often, consider writing your speed in
                                                                                           squares on your character sheet.
